item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because there's over 5,000 SCP entries now, lol. It's too much for people to read everything. It has made its slow and steady progress upwards over the years, but it has definitely slowed in the last two. Glad you appreciate it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How has this not reached 100 yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this is quite the odd duck to me. A physics based scip that is both engaging and understandable to most people reading it. I'm generally not a huge fan of physics related scips but this one mixed the science with a fantastic 'from beyond' element and then topped it off with a great set of incident logs that drew it all together. I enthusiastically +1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks very much. This is actually my personal favorite SCP I've written, so I appreciate that :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is perhaps my favorite SCP, the one I judge others by, inexplicable, utterly alien, out of context not only to the world but all other SCPs, obeys it's it's own self consistent rules but no others and distressing in some strange way. I alao like it's uniqu containment procediures because they're unique to it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For image team purposes: the image used for SCP-1350 is a modified/photoshopped version of the following image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pole-from-air.jpg which is in the public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "light" would pass through the retina from behind, but would not "illuminate" it, no. The "light" it casts does not reflect off of anything, it passes through unimpeded. How it is then perceived by anything (especially things like cameras) is a part of the questions surrounding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does this not illuminate the retina from behind when they are facing away? Or does it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point and something I had not considered. I did acknowledge it would be visible to anyone and anything that was in total darkness that was able to distinguish a point of light thousands of miles away, but I was relying on the idea that none of those instruments would be pointing toward the ground, especially not in the right direction. But that is an assumption on my part and I could be completely off-base. So if there are instruments doing exactly that, then yes, an information suppression campaign would be necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lots of food for thought here!

I did catch one thing that might be worth addressing. Information containment would be complicated. With the inverse square law, you don't have to worry about people spotting it, but there are plenty of devices and experiments worldwide that take place in near-absolute darkness, so as to detect very small amounts of light. If this SCP is detectable by machines, it should be interfering with every such experiment on Earth (regardless of shielding used). So I'd think that a TF would need to be covering this up full time. Likewise, coordination with the SCP-1350 team would be needed in the containment of any SCP that needs complete and total darkness.

From previous comments, I'm sure Rhett knows his physics (and by an even greater degree, math) far better than me, so feel free to correct me if I'm wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe the naxxramas safety dance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you brought that up, it's a point worth discussing.

If I meant exponential decrease, I'd have said exponential decrease. The report is literal on this - the decrease in base speed is linear, in that it decreases by 1.14% of its base speed after its last victim. Yes, this means that the higher its base speed is, the greater the increments by which it proceeds to decrease day by day(base speed is 100kph? Base speed decreases by 1.14kph per day. Base speed is 1000kph after the last victim? Base speed decreases by 11.4kph per day). It is NOT a decrease of 1.14% of its current speed each day.

The increase in base speed, however, is indeed exponential. Its base speed increases by exactly 22.7% per victim…..meaning that for a single victim, it will only take 17 days for it to return to its original base speed. Additional victims will increase this time period. But no matter how high its base speed ends up being, it takes no longer than 88 days for it to return to its original base speed. Additional victims will alter this of course

Thanks for mentioning it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I finally get (insofar as it can be gotten) what you were alluding to here. An enthusiastic (though still good and baffled) +1 to you, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If something is decreasing at a rate of 1.14% per day, it's by definition not decreasing linearly, it's decreasing exponentially.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has grown on me, in part after seeing your post on the Leaks page. It's just… really weird. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's minor shading, rotation, and shadow differences. you'd have to pull it up in photoshop and cut them out individually and compare to really see it. it's enough to make it clear theyre all the same TYPE of dome, but possibly not the same one. but they are in fact all the same one, and it's clear from what you said that they do look too similar. i'll see what i can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pictures are important, I agree, I just don't see the differences between domes (especially when they all seem to be in the same pose/position.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that's fair. all in all i do not feel it was a terrible photoshopping job, but i wasnt completely satisfied with it either, and dont really disagree with you. i had a terrible time finding anything that actually WOULD work in the picture. but nobody raised an objection to it before now, so i thought perhaps i was ok.

if you inspect closely you'll see there's certain key differences between the domes to differentiate them in appearance. but they were all contrived solely for the purpose of making them less obviously copy pasted. i'm not averse to revising it if i can find better looking domes to use. (oddly, the dome that looks the most fake/photoshopped to me is the main dome, even though i didn't do ANYTHING to alter that)

what i worried about more was that people would call me out on using the single most well known antarctic station as my photo, the Amundsen–Scott South Pole Station. but nobody even mentioned that :P

what i REFUSE to do is post an article without any kind of picture at all though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only real issue I have with this SCP is the picture, I know it's a stupid/petty issue but if I can't enjoy the picture then I can't immerse myself in the concept.

The problem with the image is the D-class domes, they do not match the surroundings, they do not interact with the scenery like they should, they are all copy pastes of a singular image, they do not look believable. The top middle dome in particular cuts through a shadow/structure of some kind, the shadows on all the domes do not match the shadow portrayed by the other objects or the shading on the domes themselves.

It's just not believable and if someone tried to tell me that it was an untouched/real picture I would tell them to see a psychiatrist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That does change things…gonna think on it a bit, thank you for the clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i have a theme going in SCP articles i write, and it is one i intend to maintain: violating the laws of physics…preferably in ways different than other articles do.

this thing goes against the rules of acceleration, increases supposed energy output exponentially(even though the potential energy inputs are seemingly linear), accelerates even WITHOUT energy input, reaches C, operates under baffling and complex rules for how it moves, and, as you say, should have wiped us out within its first couple days on earth.

just recognize that none of those things were oversights on my part. they were all intentional aspects of the object, intended to raise those questions in those who paid attention. not that i expect this to endear you to it or earn an upvote, but i'd rather you not get the wrong idea :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was tonally spot-on and engaging. Also, it reminds me of Robotron 2084 for some reason.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes for an interesting thought experiment, but the hollywood starfighter physics are driving me nuts. It also has what i think of as the "Bootstrapping Bugs" problem, perhaps best paraphrased as "How did we manage to contain it before it killed us?"

In fairness, this article does try to explain at least part of that. Still no vote from me, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah basically, it means "a platform that is able to rotate in order to keep a D-class facing toward/away from the object"

And thanks :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off; I like this. I like how it screws with physics and anything we know about it.

Just for my understanding: "…onto a rotating platform designed to prevent movement of the head more than 10° in any direction."
At first I thought you meant a constantly rotating platform, but that wouldn't make sense. I take it you mean a platform that keeps the head within a 10° margin of movement so to speak?

Anyway, this is a great SCP, I enjoyed it, and it gets my +1 for sure. (And for those who roam the forum: this one would be just perfect for a "document leak")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
haha. yes. yes he did.

i'll be writing a few more soon too. hopefully one more before the end of the month, but this one was the goal. i up and declared that no matter what i was going to post an SCP sometime in July.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rhett wrote an SCP?!

Also upvoted because I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
questions i hope for the reader to be left thinking of the possibilities for:

how exactly do life signs cease when it contacts the brain? what does it actually do to them?

what are the possible origins for something that behaves like this? what IS it even, given its violation of various natural laws of the universe?

events 1350-14, 1350-27, and 1350-79 are mentioned. what happened in events 1 - 13, 15 - 27, 30 - 78, and 80+(if any)?

why does eye contact matter, and how does it know someone is looking at it if theyre a long distance away and/or inside a structure?

and, one of the most important to me: what was it like for the mobile task force trying to contain it initially? what happened in the events leading to the deaths of different members? what all did they have to do to stay ahead of it? unfortunately that's a tale that i would not feel sufficient to write at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so is that an illusion created by the luminescence of what is effectively a point charge?

yes, actually :P the word "apparent" was very intentional there.

To clarify! It does not actually have a measurable diameter, no. Nor could the foundation easily get such measurements safely anyway. But it does LOOK like it is a certain size, and so they have recorded that, finding it noteworthy that it scales upward in what the volume of something that apparent size would be at the same percentage rate as speed and luminescence.

this is noteworthy in that with the scaling of the luminescence you'd think that it would appear larger much faster than that.

This is actually one of the shakier parts of the article to me, but I liked it when I thought of it, so I included it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, and have upvoted - it's subtle and manages to answer most of the questions that come to mind with a second reading. One quibble:

"SCP-1350 is a floating, mobile point of light of unknown origins"
"the object reaches a minimum apparent diameter of .5cm"

Wondering how a point of light could have a diameter: you do say 'apparent' diameter, so is that an illusion created by the luminescence of what is effectively a point charge? Personally I'd suggest 'floating, mobile luminescent body', though ymmv :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved from the draft I saw. Upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doesn't stop moving. It stops going after the closest organism looking at it and instead only goes after the closest organism until it has contacted three. After that it goes after the closest one observing it again. It's a matter of losing one D-class a month instead of three, and also not endangering containment due to the temporary imbalance.

Kappa events are mostly just a nuisance but they have the potential to make things much worse if they happen at an inopportune time. They basically happen because it starts to get "hungry" enough not to care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After the part about its light shining through all material, I started thinking, "Wait… even people?"

That's kind of scary, to be honest.

My one question is about the Kappa event. It doesn't sound like a bad thing. Let it eat enough brains and then it stops moving; what's so bad about that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand the idea you're going for, but to me, it breaks tone entirely to have the containment protocols act as though acceleration and increase in speed are the same. What's actually happening with the object, as far as the scientists are concerned, is that it has a highly highly variable acceleration, ranging from .008 m/s2 to, to you the example you used earlier where the target drove by the object, 4.5e13 m/s2, or more (calculated using a picosecond, since that's the closest to instantaneous they'd probably feel comfortable measuring). There is no such thing as acceleration without being affected by direction, from a physics standpoint. Could the object do that? Certainly, but that's at least as much of an anomaly as the eating people's brains, and should be noted as such in the procedures. As written, someone's going to come up with the same idea as me, and try it, and end up feeding D-Class to the thing because nobody bothered to tell him that the procedures neglected to mention the most important part of the object when it comes to containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, that makes sense.
All geeky, baseless objections withdrawn and I now like it even more than I did before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i understand, and that's fine.

this was an aspect of it that i envisioned before i even started writing the article(before i thought up containment procedures even), so i intend to retain it, and dont find it self contradictory.

.008m/s² on average IS a very slow acceleration, barely even noticeable. but it is cumulative to the object's total speed, not its speed in any given direction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I guess I'm happy I could do that for you? Right now, the fact that it violates its own rules (namely, solely accelerating at a slow speed), solely so that it's harder to contain makes me dislike it, and downvote. That's just my opinion, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: actually at that point i figured that i had made the article complex enough already and that trying to explain that concept would go over people's heads and not improve the article. i just didnt realize that someone would raise this question quite that quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …But what I'm saying is that that's not how physics works. Acceleration applies to both direction and distance, and there is no such thing as an object that can accelerate infinitely in direction but not in overall speed, which is what you're describing. If that is indeed how the object behaves, then you need to note that it increases its overall speed at the rate described, and then violates every law of math and physics if required to change direction. At which point, there shouldn't be any Containment Tracks involved at all, since changing direction would only slow down the vehicle being used to escape. Basically, it's not physical laws involved in the fact that it should be constrained by movement in a circle… it's mathematical ones. And if it violates those, then it's important to note that in the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not quite. let me explain it a bit better. only a certain amount of energy is emitted in any direction. in a laser, all the light is travelling in a straight line together, or at least as straight as the manufacturer is capable of making it. in light emitted in a sphere around an object, the further away from the object, the less light is present in any area of space. it's the same amount of light, but it's spread across a greater surface area.

surface area is A=4πr². a sphere that is, say, two feet wide will have a surface area of about 12.56 sq ft. a sphere that is four feet wide will have a surface area of 50.24 sq ft, exactly 4x as much surface area as the two foot ball. so light that traveled one foot from the light source would be spread across 1/4th as much area as light that traveled two feet from the light source.

if it's the same amount of light, then how can it illuminate four times as much area equally? it cant. thus 1/4th as much light falls on any equivalent area. and so on to infinity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But doesn't inverse-square have to do with apparant size rather than intensity? We've left my real-world physics knowledge behind 2 posts ago, so if you say I'm way off base I'll consider it education, but to my sci-fi-oversaturated sensibilities, apparant size would diminish with distance but light intensity would not. So at ten feet it looks like a ball the size it is shining with a certain brightness, at ten thousand miles it looks like a tiny dot that shines at the exact same intensity, in Beijing it looks like the most miniscule speck that shines equally brightly and moves in a roughly circular pattern on the ground.

Something like an omni-directional laser.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ah, but there's where the misunderstanding lies in the nature of how i wanted this thing to be. the acceleration applies to its total speed. it only goes back to its base speed upon selecting a new target. traveling in a circle vs traveling in a straight line does not affect its total speed. say that the target boarded a vehicle and traveled straight toward the object at 200mph while the object was travelling 50 mph, passing within a few feet of the object. the object would not then slow to a halt and begin accelerating from its base speed toward the target - it would continue after them at 50mph, plus its additional speed it has gained from acceleration during that time, say 51mph now instead. it is not a physical object affected by the physical world in that way.

so in chasing after a monorail, regardless of it travelling in a circle, if it was initially travelling at 4kph, after about an hour of pursuit it will be travelling at about 130kph(the normal acceleration is closer to 30m/s²). it only resets to its current base speed upon a change in targets.

otherwise yes, containment would have been a breeze. this is why the foundation had so many casualties in their initial attempts at containing it. the whole trick lies in having to change targets every so often, and doing so effectively.

*edit* also we're both using the wrong speed there. accelerating at 170m/s² would mean that after an hour it was travelling 612 kilometers per second. it will EVENTUALLY reach that speed, but not quite that fast :P the proper highest acceleration then would be about .047m/s², with the average being around .008 - .009m/s², which would translate to around an additional 100 - 120 kph per hour.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But wouldn't it be easier to just state "the object has variable acceleration, but has not been observed to go faster than 170 m/s2"? It accomplishes the same goal, except in more scientific terminology. And makes it easier to parse, or would for me.

And I think you're misunderstanding the physics involved. If it's accelerating at 170 m/s2, then your biggest worry would actually be it falling behind the monorail, as 170 m/s2 would just be enough acceleration for it to keep up with the monorail. See, when an object is going in a circle, it has to apply acceleration inwards to maintain the circular motion. And if it catches up to the D-Class… well, that's why you have secondary, smaller tracks calculated against it's base speed + 22.7% (and that plus 22.7%, and that plus 22.7% and so on and so forth). Since its maximum acceleration doesn't change, you'd actually need to go in a smaller circle to maintain distance from it. Basically, by going in a circle of the appropriate radius, you'd be able to negate its acceleration easily, much the same way you are now by making it change direction, except instead of sending it back and forth like a ping pong ball, you'd be leading it in a circle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i concede that m/s is probably a better choice, yes. however i wanted it clear that it does not always accelerate at the same rate, nor even a constant rate, but rather that that is the fastest it has been observed to accelerate. i could have given an average though i suppose.

second point, i actually considered that first. in fact, that is essentially the purpose of Containment Track A. but what do you do if, against your expectations, it catches it? plus, if it is only pursuing a single monorail it is constantly accelerating, meaning eventually it will travel faster than the monorail. basically in the end i felt that there was far more potential for things to go wrong in keeping it solely on tracks. this method requires maintenance, but has failsafes built in just in case things go wrong. if it unexpectedly catches a D-class, it is led to Track A until things are fixed up again. so long as no more than about 6 of the D-class are caught by it, containment can proceed as planned without interruption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, let me say that I enjoy the concept of the object quite a bit. However, I have a few nitpicks about the physics and math involved.

You have the acceleration listed as 10 km/hr*min. To me, that seems like sloppy science on behalf of the foundation, considering how much conversion you have to go through to put that in a form that's typically used, and actually useful for calculations, such as m/s2. To me, it would make more sense for the foundation to put the measurement in a more scientific form, so the people calculating containment don't have to whip out their calculators and do five operations before they know that it's accelerating at around 170 m/s2.

Also, it seems you could simply use circular motion to contain this far more easily than currently. A monorail track of around 140 m radius would keep the object continuously accelerating at the rate specified without ever gaining on said monorail. Granted, you'd probably need at least two monorails set up, so you can have one stop for maintenance, but the general idea is there. Heck, you could probably keep it in orbit for an indefinite amount of time by setting up your orbit properly and figuring out how to feed and keep the attention of the D-Class up there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Visibility - inverse square law. if it were a laser then yes, it'd be (nearly) just as visible. but the light is spreading out in an ever expanding sphere. the rule is that if you double the distance, you divide the amount of light hitting a certain area by 4. triple the distance, divide by 9, etc. the nearest possible observer(given where Containment Area-52 is) would be over 500 miles away. the point of light shines brightly, but measures only .5cm across. i argue that at its current brightness it would be visible from as much as 10 miles away, but 500 miles away only 1/2500 this amount of light would be striking the eye of the observer.

worst case space launch: you've summed up exactly what the foundation's problem is with the scenario, yes. the point is that this will have become the best option, as terrible as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, ocean makes sense, lots of fish in the sea and all that. Don't know why that wasn't clicking in my head for some reason.

Visibility- Keep in mind that I am no expert here. But it seems to me that a light which does not interact with matter would be exactly as visible from a few feet away as it would be from several thousand miles. If there is no scattering effect from refraction/reflection off of air, there is no reason for this light to ever disipate. It's like it is permanently in a vacumn, and in space astronauts can spot the blinking lights of other man-made objects from hundreds of miles away with the naked eye.

For the worst-case space-launch, I get that moving it away is the primary goal, and that it would come back down eventually makes sense. But what concerns me is that there would be no way to predict where it would come down. Once it gets up there, it becomes a moving, constantly shining light source against the blackness of space. That is going to turn some ehads in the astronomers guild, if not make the national new services. Once all these scientists and amateurs start observing the "newly discovered comet" or whatever they think it is, it gets pulled around like a tug-of-war with hundreds of players. On the other hand, I don't have any suggestions for a better "if all else fails" contingency, so it amounts to nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
because if it neared the ocean it would have no end of possible targets and would approach the speed of light very quickly. where it is, there's no life to interfere, and containment can proceed normally.

and yes. it is potentially visible from anywhere on earth. however, it is not bright enough at the levels currently maintained by containment to be seen from more than a few miles away. allowed to progress past a certain point…this would eventually cease to be the case.

worst case scenario containment procedure is intended to get it away from earth until it returns to a manageable speed. it is, however, guaranteed to eventually return to earth, due to that being the closest source of life massing greater than 2488 grams, and the inevitability of it catching up to the individuals that were launched into space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…why must it be kept away from the oceans?
Please, have pity or this will drive me nuts for days.

I really, really like this. But I have two quibbles, and it might just be me misundertanding.

First, If the light doesn't reflect and is visible through anything, doesn't that mean that it can be seen from anywhere on Earth? People in China see this tiny little beam of light shining straight up through the ground and moving in a circle. I don't think that it would be possible to contain knowledge of this thing, as-is. Maybe add something about the light atenuating over distance despite its lack of refraction/reflection?

Second, the worst case scenario containment procedure seems to be to launch it into space. This seems like a terrible idea to me. It would be like shooting a flare that never burns out, it's pretty much gauranteed to be seen and paid attention to by a vast number of civilians, and then all hope of knowing where it will go is out the window.

Still, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is…freaky. Not so much because of what it does, but because it can't be stopped, and the only way we're containing it is by leading it with proper bait to a place where we want it to be. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yes.

If it remains in this state for more than 24.2 days, it will cease to be influenced by the direct observation criteria until a minimum of three Delta events have occurred - this is classified as a Kappa event.

i agree on the moving parts thing, but when thinking up containment procedures these were the only ones that came to mind as viable for keeping it contained properly. this doesnt mean it's impossible for there to be a better system though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was the Kappa event ever explicitly defined?

Regardless, this comes across as one of those objects whose sole punchline is that it's difficult to contain (the fallen star implication notwithstanding). That would only bother me a little on its own, but there are so many failure points in the containment scheme as to be ridiculous. With that many moving parts, the maintenance required would rival most theme parks. Moreover, unless intelligence or mass is a factor, why are we using D-class instead of some smaller animal with a long lifespan? I'll give some thought to how containment could be simplified. In the mean time, it just bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
adjusted based on feedback.

*edit*

For image team purposes: the image used for SCP-1350 is a modified/photoshopped version of the following image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pole-from-air.jpg which is in the public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i want to note that the main idea to this object isnt about it killing people….

it's that you can always see it if you're facing toward it. even if there's stuff in the way. even in a dark enclosed room. even with your eyes closed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: haha. oddly enough, though i never have played WoW, i just realized i know exactly what you are talking about. and yeah, i suppose it would be a bit like that - except constantly moving faster the more targets it reaches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. The containment procedures do sound like a complex raid boss in World of Warcraft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SERIOUSLY. I saw the early drafts of this and liked the idea, and am glad to see that you pulled it off. But those expungements are giving me hemorrhoids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally, an SCP that actually requires an attention span in order to understand. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i actually agree with you, and that was my primary concern while posting it. but i couldn't figure anything else i could really trim out of the containment procedures while still covering everything that needed covering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes me think of Diablo II and having to outrun those damned Bone Spirit attacks, or whatever it was. Except this one just keeps going and going and going…
